locked_facts: Newton's law: dT/dt = -k(T - T_s) where T_s is surroundings temperature
Valid only when temperature difference is small (not too far from surroundings)
Cooling rate is proportional to temperature difference with surroundings
The cooling curve is exponential — body never reaches T_s exactly
Larger k = faster cooling (depends on surface area, material, convection)
For large temperature differences, Stefan's law (T⁴) is more accurate
truth_statements: Newton's law of cooling states that the rate of heat loss is proportional to the temperature difference between the body and its surroundings
The temperature approaches the surrounding temperature exponentially but theoretically never reaches it exactly
The cooling constant k depends on the nature of the body's surface, its area, and the convection conditions
Newton's law is an approximation valid for small temperature differences — for large differences radiation follows T⁴
A hot cup of coffee cools faster initially and slows down as it approaches room temperature

aha_moment.what_to_show: Two cooling curves: a hot object at 90°C and another at 60°C, both in a 25°C room. The hotter object cools faster initially (steeper slope) because the temperature difference is larger. Both curves are exponential and approach 25°C asymptotically.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Two exponential decay curves with tangent lines showing steeper slope at higher temperature difference
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students think cooling rate is constant — seeing the exponentially decaying curve and the connection between temperature difference and cooling rate is the key insight

simulation_states.label: Cooling observation
simulation_states.description: A hot body placed in a room. Temperature drops rapidly at first, then slows down. dT/dt = -k(T-T_s): cooling rate ∝ temperature difference.
simulation_states.emphasis: The bigger the temperature gap, the faster it cools

simulation_states.label: Exponential curve
simulation_states.description: Temperature vs time plotted. Exponential decay: T(t) = T_s + (T₀-T_s)e^(-kt). The curve approaches T_s asymptotically. Never quite reaches surroundings temperature.
simulation_states.emphasis: Exponential decay — approaches but never reaches surrounding temperature

simulation_states.label: Effect of initial temperature
simulation_states.description: Two objects at different initial temperatures cooling in the same room. Hotter one has steeper initial slope. Both approach T_s. The one closer to T_s cools slower.
simulation_states.emphasis: Hotter object cools faster because ΔT is larger — cooling rate is proportional to ΔT

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student sets initial temperature and surroundings temperature. Cooling curve builds in real time. Adjust k (material/surface properties). Predict temperature after given time.
simulation_states.emphasis: Set conditions and watch the cooling curve. Higher ΔT = faster cooling.

common_misconceptions.belief: Objects cool at a constant rate
common_misconceptions.correction: Cooling rate decreases over time because dT/dt ∝ (T-T_s). As the object cools and approaches T_s, the temperature difference shrinks, so cooling slows down. The curve is exponential, not linear.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show tangent lines at different points on the cooling curve — slope decreases over time

common_misconceptions.belief: The body eventually reaches the temperature of the surroundings in finite time
common_misconceptions.correction: Mathematically, the exponential decay means the body asymptotically approaches T_s but never exactly reaches it. In practice, the difference becomes negligibly small after ~5 time constants.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Zoom into the tail of the curve — it approaches T_s but never quite touches it
